text.title: Onboarding Program Design Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every repeatable onboarding program must satisfy before standing up across role families.

rule.statement: The onboarding program MUST name a single accountable owner (handle + email) and a named manager-of-record per role family. "HR" or "the team" as owner are rejected; orphan programs decay within one quarter.
rule.rationale: Plural ownership means no ownership; named accountability is how the program gets revised when hiring velocity, remote ratio, or eNPS data shifts. Without a named owner the program ages out invisibly.

rule.statement: The program MUST cover all five phases: preboarding, Day 1 orientation, 30-day check, 60-day check, 90-day formal review. Programs missing any phase are rejected as gappy; phases collapsed into one ("we just do 90 days") are rejected.
rule.rationale: Each phase covers a different anchor (Compliance, Clarification, Culture, Connection) and a missing phase predictably surfaces as a specific failure mode (Day 1 chaos, 30-day disengagement, 90-day surprise underperformance) months later.

rule.statement: Every new hire MUST be paired with a named buddy (peer, not manager) before Day 1, and the pairing recorded in the HRIS. Unpaired hires are surfaced in the weekly onboarding-health report; "implicit" buddies are rejected.

rationale.source: employee experience research / Buffer + Microsoft onboarding studies

rule.rationale: Named buddy pairings raise 90-day retention and shorten time-to-productivity; implicit buddies pair only the socially confident hires and silently disadvantage the rest. Recording the pairing makes the gap visible.

rule.statement: 30-day, 60-day, and 90-day surveys MUST roll up by cohort (hire month) into the quarterly onboarding-health report. Individual survey responses ship to the named owner; cohort roll-ups ship to leadership. Surveys that do not roll up are rejected as ceremonial.
rule.rationale: Per-hire surveys with no roll-up generate sympathy but no system change; cohort roll-ups surface the systemic friction points (equipment, doc gaps, manager pattern) that recur and let the program owner allocate fixes.

rule.statement: For remote or hybrid hires, the program MUST ship asynchronous equivalents of every synchronous Day-1 ritual (welcome lunch → async welcome thread; office tour → recorded walkthrough; ad-hoc desk visits → scheduled buddy calls). Remote hires receiving only the in-office program are rejected at audit.
rule.rationale: Remote hires onboarded through in-office rituals report 2x lower belonging at 90 days; explicit async equivalents are the cheap fix and the audit is how the program owner catches drift back to office-default.

rule.statement: If the Applies-If preconditions fail OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or to no methodology at all).
rule.rationale: Mis-application of a methodology produces worse artefacts than no methodology at all because downstream consumers trust the schema and act on bad data. The skip-record makes the non-application traceable so future audits can detect drift.
